items.prompt_md: In the sorted two-pointer scan, `numbers[lo] + numbers[hi]` is **less than** the target. Why does moving `hi` left instead of `lo` right fail to fix this?
options: numbers[hi] is already the largest value left, so pairing it with anything smaller can only shrink the sum further, moving away from the target.
Moving hi left would skip over the correct answer entirely, even though the sum would increase.
The array might not actually be sorted, so hi's position is unreliable.
Moving hi left changes the array's length, which invalidates lo.
items.answer: numbers[hi] is already the largest value left, so pairing it with anything smaller can only shrink the sum further, moving away from the target.
items.explanation_md: `numbers[hi]` is the biggest value still in range. Pairing it with anything smaller than `numbers[lo]` only makes the sum smaller — the opposite of what an undersized sum needs. Only `lo` moving right can raise the sum.

items.prompt_md: `numbers[lo] + numbers[hi]` is **greater than** the target. Which pointer should move, and why?
options: hi moves left, because numbers[hi] is the largest value in range and dropping it is the only way to reduce an oversized sum.
lo moves right, because numbers[lo] is too small and needs to increase.
Both pointers move at once so the pair stays centered.
Neither pointer moves; the target should be recomputed instead.
items.answer: hi moves left, because numbers[hi] is the largest value in range and dropping it is the only way to reduce an oversized sum.
items.explanation_md: By symmetric logic to the too-small case: `numbers[hi]` is the largest value left, so it's the only element whose removal can shrink an oversized sum.

items.prompt_md: Ignoring the cost of sorting, what is the time complexity of the two-pointer scan itself for the sorted pair-sum problem?
items.explanation_md: Every pointer move discards exactly one candidate pair from further consideration, and `lo`/`hi` can move at most n times combined before they cross — a single O(n) pass.

items.prompt_md: Including the cost of sorting an initially unsorted array, what is the total time complexity of solving pair-sum with two pointers?
items.explanation_md: The sort dominates: O(n log n) to sort plus O(n) for the scan is still O(n log n) overall.

items.prompt_md: What is the extra space complexity of the two-pointer pair-sum scan, beyond whatever space the sort itself uses?
items.explanation_md: The scan only tracks two index variables, `lo` and `hi` — no hash map or auxiliary array is needed, unlike the unsorted-array approach.

items.prompt_md: For 3-sum (find a triple summing to target) on a sorted array, what's the standard two-pointer strategy?
options: Fix one element with an outer loop, then run the sorted two-pointer pair-sum scan on the remaining subarray for the other two elements.
Run three independent pointers that each start at a different position in the array.
Hash every pair sum and look up the third element for each starting index.
Binary search for each of the three elements independently.
items.answer: Fix one element with an outer loop, then run the sorted two-pointer pair-sum scan on the remaining subarray for the other two elements.
items.explanation_md: 3-sum reduces to pair-sum: fix the first element, then reuse the same converging two-pointer idea on the rest of the sorted array to find the other two.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the fix-one-and-two-pointer approach to 3-sum on a sorted array (after the initial sort)?
items.explanation_md: The outer loop runs O(n) times, and each iteration runs an O(n) two-pointer scan, giving O(n) * O(n) = O(n^2).

items.prompt_md: In 3-sum, after fixing the first element of a triple, why do you skip past duplicate values before advancing the fixed index to the next iteration?
options: So the same triple of values isn't reported more than once when the array contains repeated numbers.
Because duplicate values break the sortedness invariant the two pointers rely on.
To keep the array's length constant during the scan.
Because duplicate values always sum to the target and must be excluded.
items.answer: So the same triple of values isn't reported more than once when the array contains repeated numbers.
items.explanation_md: If the fixed index lands on the same value as the previous iteration, it would rediscover the exact same set of triples again — skipping duplicates keeps the output unique.

items.prompt_md: Inside the inner two-pointer scan of 3-sum, after finding a valid triple, why do you also skip duplicate values at `lo` and `hi` before continuing?
options: To avoid re-reporting the same triple again when adjacent elements are equal.
Because the inner scan would otherwise run in O(n^2) time instead of O(n).
To make sure lo and hi never point at the same index.
Because equal values always mean the current sum is now wrong.
items.answer: To avoid re-reporting the same triple again when adjacent elements are equal.
items.explanation_md: If `numbers[lo]` equals the value just used, moving `lo` by one without skipping would immediately rebuild the identical triple — skipping duplicates at both pointers keeps every reported triple distinct.

items.prompt_md: A brute-force pair-sum check on an unsorted array tries every pair, costing O(n^2). Why is it worth paying O(n log n) to sort first and then use two pointers?
options: O(n log n) sort plus O(n) scan is asymptotically cheaper than O(n^2) once n is reasonably large, and it uses O(1) extra space unlike a hash map.
Sorting is required by definition before any array can be searched for a pair.
It isn't actually worth it; brute force is always faster in practice.
Sorting removes duplicate values so the pair can't be miscounted.
items.answer: O(n log n) sort plus O(n) scan is asymptotically cheaper than O(n^2) once n is reasonably large, and it uses O(1) extra space unlike a hash map.
items.explanation_md: O(n log n) beats O(n^2) for large n, and unlike the hash-map approach it does so without O(n) extra space — sortedness lets the array itself do the pruning work.

items.prompt_md: A buggy implementation moves `lo` right when the sum is too big and moves `hi` left when the sum is too small — the moves are swapped from the correct version. What happens?
options: The scan can skip past the correct pair or fail to converge, because each move now pushes the sum in the wrong direction.
It still works correctly, just in a different order.
The time complexity becomes O(n log n) instead of O(n).
It only fails when the target itself is negative.
items.answer: The scan can skip past the correct pair or fail to converge, because each move now pushes the sum in the wrong direction.
items.explanation_md: Swapping the moves makes the sum drift further from the target instead of closer to it, so the loop can miss the matching pair or bounce without ever narrowing the range correctly.

items.prompt_md: What condition keeps the sorted two-pointer pair-sum loop running, and what does it mean when that condition becomes false?
options: The loop runs while lo < hi; once they meet or cross, every possible pair has been considered and no match remains to check.
The loop runs while lo == hi; once they differ, the search is complete.
The loop runs a fixed n^2 number of times regardless of pointer positions.
The loop runs until numbers[lo] equals numbers[hi] in value, not position.
items.answer: The loop runs while lo < hi; once they meet or cross, every possible pair has been considered and no match remains to check.
items.explanation_md: Each iteration eliminates one whole candidate pair; once `lo` and `hi` meet or cross, the shrinking range is empty and there is nothing left to check.

items.prompt_md: An array is already sorted in ascending order. You need to return any single pair of values that sums exactly to a target, using no extra memory proportional to the array size.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Sorted plus pair-target is the trigger for converging two pointers — the comparison against the target tells you which whole range of pairs to discard, in O(1) extra space.

items.prompt_md: An array is unsorted, and the answer must be the two numbers' *original indices* — sorting would destroy the mapping back to those positions.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Unsorted plus indices-that-must-be-preserved rules out sorting. A hash map of seen values to their original index finds the complement in O(n) without disturbing order.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, find every unique triplet of values that sums to zero, with no duplicate triplets in the output.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.answer: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: 3-sum on a sorted array reduces to pair-sum: fix one value with an outer loop, then converge two pointers over the remainder, skipping duplicates as you go.

items.prompt_md: You have an unsorted list of transaction amounts and must find, by their original position in the list, two transactions that cancel out to a specific refund amount.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Unsorted data where original position matters is the hashing trigger — sorting to enable two pointers would lose the index information the answer needs.

items.prompt_md: A sorted array holds a million integers. You need to determine whether any pair sums to a target value without allocating memory that scales with the array size.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Already sorted plus an O(1)-space requirement is exactly what converging two pointers is for — no hash map needed.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, count how many pairs of elements sum to strictly less than a given target.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: The sorted-plus-target comparison still applies: when a pair's sum is small enough, every pair between the current lo and hi also qualifies, letting two pointers count in O(n).

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, find the triple of values whose sum is closest to a target — not necessarily equal to it.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.answer: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Same shape as 3-sum: fix one element, then converge two pointers over the rest, tracking the best sum seen instead of stopping at an exact match.

items.prompt_md: A sorted array contains duplicate values. You need every distinct pair of positions whose values sum to a target, without reporting the same value-pair twice.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Still sorted plus pair-target — the only addition is skipping over repeated values at lo and hi after a match so the same value-pair isn't reported again.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array of distinct positive integers, find a pair of values whose *difference* equals a target value.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: The array is still sorted, and each pointer move is still decided by comparing the current pair against the target — only the comparison (difference vs. sum) changes, not the pattern.

items.prompt_md: A sorted list of item prices is given. Find the two items whose combined price comes as close as possible to a budget without exceeding it.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Sorted plus a sum-vs-budget comparison is the same converging-pointer shape, just optimizing 'closest without going over' instead of an exact match.

items.prompt_md: A sorted array is small, and duplicate values are common. As you scan for pairs summing to a target, you also need to advance past a repeated value at lo before continuing, so the same match isn't found twice.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: The array is sorted and the task is still pair-sum — duplicate-skipping is a refinement of the same converging two-pointer scan, not a different pattern.

items.prompt_md: Numbers arrive in an unsorted stream and are loaded into an array. You must report all pairs, by their original index in that array, that sum to a target — and you cannot sort, because the indices must map back to the input order.
options: Fix one element and two-pointer the rest
items.explanation_md: Unsorted input where indices must be preserved is the classic hashing trigger — a hash map of value to index finds every complement without disturbing the original order.
